spec_match.rationale: Shows AccentColor wired through a full task-app mockup (titlebar, nav, badges, checkboxes, buttons, focus ring) which exercises the spec's color contexts well, but the actual installed-PWA gating that triggers the keyword's resolution is only mentioned in the explainer prose, not demonstrated.

interactive.rationale: Color picker drives a real app surface in real time across nine elements; the script directly mutates inline styles per element rather than relying on a static screenshot.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Lede promises a simulated installed PWA whose chrome is driven by AccentColor/AccentColorText; the page delivers a recognisable task-app mock-up that updates on every input.

edge_cases.rationale: Decent coverage of patterns but no demo of failure modes — what happens on the open web (fallback), what happens in forced-colors mode, what happens with low-contrast accents (e.g. #888 picked by user). The AccentColorText computation also uses luminance>0.179 not the spec's higher-contrast rule.

design_system.rationale: Uses CSS variables throughout, focus override sets outline-color only, no static-card; the simulated app frame is bordered consistently with the rest of the site.

openQuestions.title: Add a 'not installed' state preview
openQuestions.detail: Mirror the existing app preview with a second frame labelled 'open-web fallback' that shows the keyword resolving to a neutral system color. This is the actual difference Chrome 150 ships and the demo currently doesn't show it.

openQuestions.title: Add a low-contrast accent warning
openQuestions.detail: If the user picks an accent whose computed AccentColorText still doesn't hit WCAG AA against the surface (a real risk on mid-tone hues), surface a small warning chip. Otherwise the page silently produces unreadable UI for some accent choices, which contradicts the headline 'AccentColorText is always readable' claim.

openQuestions.title: Wire forced-colors mode
openQuestions.detail: Add a 'simulate forced-colors' toggle that flips a class on the app frame to a @media (forced-colors: active) override demonstrating how AccentColor exposes the OS-chosen palette in High Contrast. Critical accessibility story that's missing.

openQuestions.title: Show window-controls-overlay integration
openQuestions.detail: If this is meant as a PWA preview, pair it with windowControlsOverlay theming — the titlebar AccentColor is also where the OS draws its window chrome. Mention the env(titlebar-area-*) variables or the controls-overlay-visible CSS pseudo so authors learn the full PWA chrome story.

summary: Convincing full-app mockup that demonstrates AccentColor in realistic UI patterns, but the install-context fallback (the actual reason the keyword shipped in 150) is left to prose rather than shown. Add an open-web fallback frame and forced-colors simulation.
